articles.headline: 90 Minutes a Week. That's the Strength-Training Longevity Number.
articles.body: A massive 30-year study tracking 147,000 people found the magic number for extending your life: 90 to 120 minutes of strength training weekly. No need to live in the gym. This amount linked to a 19 percent lower risk of cardiovascular death and 27 percent lower risk of neurological death. Push past 120 minutes? The research shows zero additional benefit. The takeaway is simple: progressive resistance training, done consistently within this window, may be one of your best longevity investments.
articles.takeaway: Aim for 90-120 minutes of strength training per week. Going harder won't give you more life extension.
articles.source: ScienceDaily / British Journal of Sports Medicine

articles.topic: Apple Watch detects heart arrhythmias better
articles.headline: Your Apple Watch Might Be Better at Heart Monitoring Than You Think
articles.body: A new study presented at Heart Rhythm 2026 found that the Apple Watch can capture irregular heartbeats in kids more effectively than traditional medical patch monitors. The research compared smartwatch sensors to standard ambulatory devices and found wearable technology holding its own in clinical accuracy. This matters because arrhythmias affect millions. If a consumer device sitting on your wrist catches what medical-grade monitors miss, that's a game changer for early detection and peace of mind.
articles.takeaway: Consumer wearables like the Apple Watch are approaching clinical accuracy for arrhythmia detection. Worth paying attention to the alerts.
articles.pexelsQuery: smartwatch heart rate monitor wrist technology

articles.headline: Four-Seven-Eight. The Breathing Pattern That Speeds Up Recovery.
articles.body: Structured breathing between your sets isn't woo. Research shows that slow breathing techniques, particularly the 4-7-8 method (inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 7, exhale for 8), activate your parasympathetic nervous system faster than just resting. One study found athletes using this technique reduced competitive anxiety by 38 percent before performance. Another showed it cuts post-exercise heart rate recovery by an additional 13 percent compared to passive rest. This is free. Takes minutes. Works.
articles.takeaway: Use 4-7-8 breathing between sets or after hard efforts to accelerate nervous system recovery and reduce lingering stress hormones.
articles.source: Supportive Care & Journal of Human Kinetics

articles.headline: Your Stiffness Isn't Just Tight Muscles. It's Your Connective Tissue.
articles.body: You stretch. You foam roll. Yet stiffness returns. The reason: traditional recovery methods target muscles in isolation, missing the broader connective tissue system underneath. Fascia, the web-like tissue surrounding muscles and organs, can lose its elasticity when exposed to static posture, repetitive patterns, or poor recovery. When fascial layers stop sliding smoothly, range of motion shrinks and movement becomes heavy. The fix isn't more stretching. It's structured myofascial release and movement variability. Address the tissue quality, not just the muscle length.
articles.takeaway: Focus on connective tissue quality through myofascial release and varied movement patterns, not isolated stretching.

quote.text: The more you move, the more you're alive. The less you move, the less you're alive.
quote.author: Peter Attia
quote.context: Longevity physician and host of The Peter Attia Drive podcast, discussing the fundamental link between movement and lifespan.

workout.title: Dip Strength Circuit: Bodyweight + Weighted Hypertrophy Density
workout.tip: On weighted dips, lean forward slightly and keep elbows tucked to 45 degrees—this shifts load to chest and triceps. Lower until elbows are 90 degrees, pause briefly, and drive up through your hands and legs. Avoid shrugging at the top.
workout.warmup: 5 min: 12 band pull-aparts, 10 scapular dips (bodyweight, slow and controlled), 8 arm circles, 10 pushups

exercises.name: Weighted Dips (Dip Belt)
exercises.notes: Add 15–25 lbs depending on baseline strength. Lower for 2 seconds, drive up explosively. Rest 2.5 min between sets. This is your strength anchor.

exercises.name: Bodyweight Dips (Tempo)
exercises.notes: After the heavy work, drop weight and perform 10 reps with 3-second negatives. Metabolic stress phase. Rest 90 sec.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Floor Press
exercises.notes: Lying on the floor limits leg drive, forcing triceps and chest to work harder. Press dumbbells above chest, squeeze at the top. Rest 75 sec.

exercises.name: Tricep Rope Pushdown (Cable Machine)
exercises.notes: High-rep, short rest. Split the rope and push down hard, squeeze triceps. Use lighter weight and focus on the muscle. Rest 45 sec.

workout.cooldown: 5 min: chest stretch on foam roller 60 sec, tricep overhead stretch 30 sec each arm, doorway pec stretch 45 sec each side, deep breathing in child's pose 30 sec
workout.description: A hybrid strength-hypertrophy block that uses dips as the primary driver of chest, shoulder, and tricep development. The circuit alternates heavy weighted dips with higher-rep assistance work, creating accumulated tension and metabolic stress—the dual pathway to muscle growth. Perfect for intermediate athletes seeking functional pressing strength and upper-body mass.

meal.title: Cod and Roasted Chickpea Grain Bowl with Lemon Tahini Drizzle
meal.description: Double the chickpea batch and store the extras. They stay crispy for 2 days and make an excellent high-protein snack or salad topper.
meal.instructions:
Ingredients:
2 cod fillets (6 oz each)
1 can (15 oz) chickpeas, drained and rinsed
1 cup cooked farro or brown rice
1 cup baby spinach or arugula
1 tbsp olive oil
1 tsp smoked paprika
1/2 tsp garlic powder
Salt and black pepper to taste
2 tbsp tahini
Juice of 1 lemon
1 tbsp water (to thin tahini dressing)
Fresh parsley to garnish (optional)

Instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 425F. Toss drained chickpeas with half the olive oil, smoked paprika, garlic powder, salt, and pepper. Spread on a baking sheet and roast 15-18 minutes until crispy, shaking halfway. 2. While chickpeas roast, pat cod dry, season with salt, pepper, and a pinch of paprika. Heat remaining olive oil in a skillet over medium-high. Cook cod 3-4 minutes per side until flaky and golden. 3. Whisk tahini, lemon juice, and water into a smooth dressing. Add more water if too thick. 4. Build bowls: grain base, greens, cod, crispy chickpeas. Drizzle with lemon tahini dressing and garnish with parsley.
